coredata.dc:description: Preparing carbon materials with both high surface area and density is crucial for improving the volumetric performance of supercapacitors. Herein, we show a novel phenomenon that chitosan can go through spontaneous densification during chemical activation, enabling the microporous carbon with large surface area and extremely high compaction density (2,007 m2 g−1, 1.21 g cm− 3). This is quite different from direct pyrolysis and/or activation of many other carbon precursors, which usually form loose structure due to the serious foaming. The mechanism is revealed as the formation of highly crosslinked and stable scaffold by the thermally induced Schiff base reaction between amino and aldehyde groups, which not only inhibits the foaming phenomenon, but also facilitates further aromatization of carbon scaffold. The optimized sample shows high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances (280 F g−1 and 339 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1) and good rate performance (58% retention at 100 A g− 1) for aqueous supercapacitors, outperforming most of the reported carbon materials. Considering the low-cost and simple process, this work may provide a new way for structural design and practical application of dense carbon materials.

head.ce:title: Schiff base reaction induced densification of chitosan-derived microporous carbon for compact capacitive energy storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microporous carbon prepared by homogeneous activation of chitosan integrates both high surface area and compaction density due to the formation of highly cross-linked and stable scaffold by thermally induced Schiff base reaction, showing good gravimetric and volumetric capacitances for supercapacitors.

Preparing carbon materials with both high surface area and density is crucial for improving the volumetric performance of supercapacitors. Herein, we show a novel phenomenon that chitosan can go through spontaneous densification during chemical activation, enabling the microporous carbon with large surface area and extremely high compaction density (2,007 m2 g−1, 1.21 g cm− 3). This is quite different from direct pyrolysis and/or activation of many other carbon precursors, which usually form loose structure due to the serious foaming. The mechanism is revealed as the formation of highly crosslinked and stable scaffold by the thermally induced Schiff base reaction between amino and aldehyde groups, which not only inhibits the foaming phenomenon, but also facilitates further aromatization of carbon scaffold. The optimized sample shows high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances (280 F g−1 and 339 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1) and good rate performance (58% retention at 100 A g− 1) for aqueous supercapacitors, outperforming most of the reported carbon materials. Considering the low-cost and simple process, this work may provide a new way for structural design and practical application of dense carbon materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Constructing interconnected microporous structure through homogeneous activation [6,7] (also known as one-step activation [8–10], in-situ activation [11,12], or ionic activation [13,14]) is verified as an effective way to simultaneously achieve large surface area, high density, and abundant ion migration channels in porous carbon [15–20]. Homogeneous activation is done by uniform adsorption/coordination of metal ions in carbon precursors, then pyrolysis at high temperature. The uniform etching of carbon scaffold leads to the formation of interconnected porous structure. For example, Zhuo et al. have finely tuned the micropores of the carboxylic phenolic resin derived porous carbons using different alkali metal ions. The Cs+ activated sample has a specific surface area of 1,312 m2 g−1 and a high compaction density of 0.92 g cm− 3, showing both high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances in KOH and ionic liquid electrolyte [21]. Through homogeneous activation of carbon dots, we have prepared several microporous carbons integrated with large surface area, high compaction density, and modulated porous structure, showing good gravimetric, volumetric, and rate performance in aqueous and organic electrolytes [15,20,22,23].

Designing well-developed micropores and inhibiting the formation of excessive meso- and macro-pores are the key points to integrate both high surface area and density of porous carbon. But as seen from Table S1 (Supporting Information), many carbon precursors such as K-containing organic salts, small molecules, and linear polymers would go through severe foaming during pyrolysis and/or homogeneous activation due to the vigorous decomposition and gas release, resulting in the derived porous carbon presenting a hierarchically porous structure with a low density. For example, the early studies of direct pyrolyzing potassium citrate could obtain activated carbons with a high surface area up to 2,200 m2 g−1 but a low density of ca. 0.30 g cm− 3, which is not suitable for compact energy storage [8,24]. To solve the foaming problem, the precursors should go through strong crosslinking to construct stable scaffold before pyrolysis/activation, as summarized in Table S2. However, these methods usually result in the consumption of the porous channels and functional groups inside the precursors, resulting in the difficulty for homogeneous adsorption of activator ions. Therefore, it is usually necessary to implement further pre-treatments (such as chemical modification and pre-oxidation methods) to introduce additional functional groups before ion activator adsorption and homogeneous activation. For example, Liu et al. proposed that adding acrylic acid in the hydrothermal carbonization of glucose could simultaneously form a highly cross-linked structure by cycloaddition and provide carboxylic groups as the K+ adsorption sites [13]. Similarly, Chen et al. reported the carboxyl-functionalization of hydrochar by pre-oxidation method, also aiming to introduce adsorption sites for activators. The large-surface-area microporous carbon with narrow pore size is prepared by ion exchange and homogeneous activation [12]. But the complex and tedious procedures obviously hinder the homogeneous activation for large-scale preparation. Therefore, finding a carbon precursor that can uniformly adsorb/coordinate activators and spontaneously form stable scaffold during homogeneous activation is of importance, yet still challenging.

Herein, we report a novel microporous carbon integrated with a large surface area of 2,007 m2 g−1 and an extremely high compaction density of 1.21 g cm− 3 prepared by directly homogeneous activation of chitosan as the precursor. Chitosan goes through spontaneous densification even under the severe activation condition. The reaction mechanism is revealed as the formation of strongly cross-linked scaffold by thermally induced Schiff base reaction between amino and aldehyde groups, which effectively inhibits the foaming phenomenon and facilitates the aromatization of carbon scaffold. Benefiting from the large surface area, high density, and interconnected ion migration channels, the sample shows high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances (280 F g−1/339 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1) and good rate performance (58% retention at 100 A g− 1), which are superior to most of the reported porous carbon materials. Moreover, the symmetric supercapacitor achieves both high energy and power densities of 10.50 Wh L−1 and 295 W L−1 at the commercial mass loading of 10 mg cm− 2, revealing high potential for dense and fast energy storage application.

Chitosan was purchased from Beijing Cool Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Deacetylation degree ≥ 95%, Mw ≈ 70,000). In a typical process, 0.5 g chitosan was completely dispersed into 80 mL diluted acetic acid solution (5 wt%) by vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 750 μL of 6 mol L−1 KOH aqueous solution (containing 0.25 g KOH) was added into the above solution, and homogeneously mixed by stirring for 6 h. After being dried at 110 °C, the mixture was milled to fine powder. The obtained powder was carbonized at 250 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1, and then calcined at 800 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min− 1. The obtained powder was immersed in 1 mol L−1 HCl solution to remove the inorganic salts, then washed to neutral using deionized water by repeated centrifugation. The obtained sample was named as CPC (chitosan-derived porous carbon).

Starch-derived porous carbon was prepared by the similar method but using corn starch as the precursor and hot water as the solvent to clarify the densification mechanism during pyrolysis. The sample was denoted as SPC (starch-derived porous carbon). The CPC-0 and SPC-0 were prepared without using KOH activator.

The samples were measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advances), thermogravimetric analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (TG-DSC, Mettler Toledo TGA/DSC1), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Bruker VERTEX 70), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi), and Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA). The specific surface area and pore size distribution were measured by a volumetric adsorption apparatus (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics). The surface area was obtained by Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The micro- and meso-pore size distribution was obtained by the Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method. The macroporous structure was measured by an automatic mercury porosimeter (Micromeritics autopore IV 9500). The electric conductivity of the sample is obtained by I-V curves [25–27].

The electrochemical performance was measured on a CHI 760E electrochemical working station using 6 mol L−1 KOH aqueous electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 85 wt% of the active material, 10 wt% carbon black, and 5 wt% polytetrafluoroethylene binder were milled to slurry using ethanol as the solvent, then pressed onto the nickel foam (coating area: 1.0 × 2.0 cm2) as the current collector with the mass loading of 2 and 10 mg cm− 2. The platinum sheet (1.5 × 1.5 cm2) and standard Hg/HgO electrode were selected as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively, in a three-electrode system. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) were performed in the potential window of −0.9–0.1 V vs. the reference. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in a frequency ranging from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The galvanostatic capacitances (Cg ) were calculated from the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves are according to the equation [28]:

As naturally-abundant and low-cost polysaccharides, chitosan and starch have the similar molecular units and double helical structure (Fig. 1 a1, b1, and S1), except that the –OH on the second carbon atom of β-pyranose unit is replaced by –NH2 [31–34]. For preparing interconnected porous carbon by homogeneous activation, chitosan and starch are uniformly mixed with a small amount of KOH, obtaining the brown and light-brown precursors, respectively. The precursors (0.7 g) were spread into a thin layer at the bottom of the alumina crucibles. After pyrolysis, the black products of chitosan and chitosan/KOH mixture show barely changed volume (Fig. 1a1 and S2a), demonstrating the non-foaming phenomenon of chitosan during pyrolysis or even chemical activation. The SEM images show that CPC-0 from direct pyrolysis of chitosan exhibits a dense block structure with a particle size of ca. 10–18 μm (Fig. 1a2). The strong etching and pore-creating during homogeneous activation make the large carbon particles easy to cleave, leading to the obtained CPC with a reduced particle size to 5.0–8.5 μm. But CPC still maintains a dense structure without large pores on its smooth surface (Fig. 1a3). In strong contrast, starch goes through serious foaming induced by the rapid decomposition and gas release during pyrolysis [35,36]. The SEM image of SPC-0 shows many thin sheets with a thickness of ca. 0.80 μm, which is due to the breaking of thin-walled carbon foam by slight grinding (Fig. 1b2). When pyrolysis of the starch/KOH mixture for homogeneous activation, more serious foaming takes place. The loose black product shows an obvious volume expansion of at least 5 times larger than the precursor because strong chemical etching further promotes the precursor decomposition and gas release (Fig. 1b1 and S2b). The obtained SPC particles have a loose structure containing many large pores on the rough surface (Fig. 1b3). What’s more, the CPC particles are easier to form a densely packed structure than the SPC particles due to their regular block morphology. Therefore, CPC shows extremely high tap density (measuring method seen in Supporting Information) and compaction density of 0.72 and 1.21 g cm− 3, respectively, nearly two times higher than the SPC (0.41 and 0.67 g cm− 3).

Precursor foaming during pyrolysis usually produces macropores in carbon materials, which is the main reason decreasing the tap and compaction densities. As measured by mercury intrusion porosimetry (Fig. 2d), CPC shows a two-stage curve containing a slight increase below 8 psia (stage 1) and a rapid intrusion to a saturated plateau at 1,000 psia (stage 2), demonstrating a single peak macropore distribution with the average size of 15.44 μm. Considering its dense block structure with the particle size of 5.0–8.5 μm, these macropores are mainly coming from the space between the packed particles, which is in consisted with the SEM image (Fig. 1a3). In contrast, SPC shows a three-stage mercury intrusion process, corresponding to the macropore sizes of 0.55 and 19.10 μm (Fig. 2e). According to the SEM images, the 0.55 μm macropores are identified as the large pores on the rough surface of the SPC particles, which are formed through serious foaming and chemical etching during homogeneous activation. The 19.10 μm macropores are mainly due to the space between the loosely stacked particles. The macropore volume of CPC (1.80 cm3 g− 1) is more than 2 times lower than that of the SPC (3.72 cm3 g− 1), confirming its high density originated from the novel non-foaming phenomenon of the chitosan precursor (Fig. 2f).

From the above results, chitosan shows a unique thermally induced densification feature during pyrolysis and/or homogeneous activation, which is different from the serious foaming of many other polysaccharides [49,50], [Table S1]. Considering the similar molecular structures of chitosan and starch except for the replacement of –OH by –NH2, we reasonably deduce that the formation of dense carbon is due to the thermochemical reactions based on the –NH2 groups. For deep understanding, we firstly measured the TG-DSC curves in N2 atmosphere. Both chitosan and starch show a slight weight-loss at 70–100 °C, then a sharp weight-loss at 270–370 °C (Fig. 3 a). The first weight-loss with an endothermic behavior (DSC results in Fig. 3b) is ascribed to the evaporation of absorbed-water and intermolecular dehydration caused by removal of the active –OH groups [36,51,52]. The sharp weight-loss in the second stage is due to the complex thermochemical reactions primary including decomposition, dehydration, and crosslinking, together with the severe gas release [53–55]. However, chitosan shows an exothermic behavior with lower weight-loss ratio (47.30%), which is quite different from starch with an endothermic behavior (61.62%). This reveals that chitosan goes through stronger crosslinking reactions (exothermic) than starch at this stage. The released heat exceeds the absorbed heat required by the decomposition and dehydration reactions (endothermic). In addition, chitosan shows barely changed volume during pyrolysis, while starch presents an obvious foaming phenomenon at 300 °C (Fig. S4). Therefore, the inhibition of foaming phenomenon is mainly due to the formation of stable cross-linked structure rather than buffering the severe gas release. Finally, chitosan and starch show a slight weight-loss with an exothermic behavior as the temperature increases from 400 to 1,000 °C, indicating the polycondensation and formation of stable carbon scaffold.

The experimental pyrolysis program is different from the TG-DSC measurement, which will generate some difference in the thermochemical reactions of the precursors (slow heating rate plays an important role in immobilizing carbon). In order to accurately elucidate the structural evolution process, we thermally treated chitosan and starch under the practical pyrolysis program to the key temperatures of 250 °C, 400 °C, and 600 °C, and measured the FT-IR spectra of the products (Fig. 3c, d, and e). Pristine chitosan and starch have the similar characteristic groups of –OH/–NH (3,400 cm− 1), aliphatic –CH– (2,900 cm− 1 and 1,450 cm− 1), and C–O–C (ca. 1,000 cm− 1). The absorption peak located at ca. 1,595 cm− 1 in chitosan is the N-H stretching vibration [56,57]. The intensities of –OH/–NH (3,400 cm− 1) and –CH– (2,900 cm− 1) peaks in both samples are weakened below 250 °C, which proves that the physically absorbed water and active hydroxyl group are decomposed into volatile water (equation (7)) [58]. The decreased intensity of C–O–C peak at 1,000–1,200 cm− 1 indicates partial decomposition of glycosidic bonds and further intramolecular dehydration [59]. The lower weight loss of chitosan than starch is possibly due to the less absorbed water and active terminal groups on the chitosan chains than starch. For pyrolyzing chitosan at 400 °C, the peaks of C–O–C, –CH–, and –NH groups are vanished, along with the rising of in-plane C=C skeletal vibration in aromatic rings at about 1,575 cm− 1 [60], respectively. The terminal groups and the unsaturated aliphatic chains are almost removed with the formation of pyrazine units through the thermally induced Schiff base reaction (equation (8)) [35,51]. When the temperature reaches 600 °C, the increasing of C–O–C peak intensity (1,100 cm− 1) indicates the further crosslinking and cyclization to form furan rings. The C=C peak shifts to a lower wave number of 1,558 cm− 1, indicating the formation of highly conjugated structure and effective immobilization of the carbon species.
(7) C-OH + HO-C → C-O-C + H2O
(8) R-NH2 + O=CH-R’ → R-N =CH-R’ + H2O

In contrast, when pyrolyzing starch at 400 °C, the presence of C=C and C-O-C peaks indicates the formation of carbon scaffold and cross-linked structure to some extent (equation (7)). But the products contain a large number of terminal –OH groups and –CH– chains even the temperature is raised above 600 °C, indicating a relatively low crosslinking degree and an unstable carbon scaffold [61]. The continuous decomposition of –OH and –CH– during pyrolysis leads to the chain breaking and gas release, accompanied by the serious foaming phenomenon [62,63].

Based on the discussion above, the reaction mechanism is given in Fig. 4 . The decomposition of chitosan macromolecule in the temperature ranging from 270 to 300 °C leads to the formation of aldehyde groups by the ring opening of glycosidic units (Step I and II). Then, the Schiff base reaction occurs between –NH2 and the as-formed aldehyde group, leading to the formation of pyrazine unit and an intensive cross-linked structure between the molecular chains (Step III and IV) [64,65]. This highly crosslinked structure not only inhibits the foaming phenomenon but also facilitates the cyclization and aromatization, promoting the formation of densified carbon skeleton for CPC even though in the homogeneous activation process.

Both CPC and SPC show weak and dispersive peaks located at ca. 23 and 43° in the XRD patterns, demonstrating their highly disordered crystal structure. A rising tail in the low-angle region of SPC is the air scattering background due to the well-developed meso- and macro-pores [15,16,66] (Fig. 5 a). The Raman spectra of SPC and CPC can be fitted into four Gaussian peaks located at ca. 1,152, 1,342, 1,500, and 1,587 cm− 1, corresponding to the T-, D-, D’-, and G-bands, respectively (Fig. 5b) [67–71]. CPC shows a higher specific surface area and abundant micropores, but a lower AD/AG ratio (1.74) than SPC under the same activated condition. In addition, the XPS spectra of CPC and SPC (Fig. 5c) show two peaks at around 284.6 and 531.0 eV, corresponding to the O1s and C1s components, respectively. The XPS C1s spectra are divided into three components of C=C (284.6 eV), C–O (286.1 eV), C=O (288.6 eV) components (Fig. 5d), respectively [2,15,72]. CPC shows higher total carbon content (89.0 at.%) and sp2 carbon component (64.5 at.%) than SPC (86.8 at.%, 60.7 at.%). The result proves that the Schiff base reaction is conducive to the formation of highly conjugated structure of CPC (Step IV in Fig. 4). Thus, despite the larger specific surface area and richer micropores, CPC has a comparable electric conductivity as SPC (40 S m− 1, measuring method seen in Supporting Information).

The capacitive energy storage performance of the samples is investigated in aqueous KOH electrolyte using three-electrode system. CPC and SPC both show slightly distorted CV curves from the rectangle shape at 10 mV s− 1 (Fig. 6 a) [73], indicating their energy storage is primarily contributed by the electrical double-layer capacitance from the large surface area, and small amount of pseudocapacitance from the heteroatom doping (nitrogen and/or oxygen, Table S3 and Fig. S8). Previously, we have proved that the oxygen components contribute to more pseudo-capacitance than the nitrogen components in aqueous KOH electrolyte [74]. In this work, the N and O total content of CPC is 11.0 at.%, lower than the O content of SPC. Therefore, we consider that the pseudocapacitance contribution from the heteroatom-doping of CPC will not be higher than that of SPC. CPC shows the gravimetric capacitances of 280, 236, 224, and 200 F g−1 at 1, 10, 20, and 50 A g− 1, respectively, which are slightly higher than SPC (258, 221, 210, and 190 F g−1). Importantly, benefiting from its extremely high compact density of 1.21 g cm− 3, CPC shows ultrahigh volumetric capacitances of 339 and 286 F cm− 3 at 1 and 10 A g− 1, respectively, as well as a good rate performance of 196 F cm− 3 at 100 A g− 1 (58% capacitance retention). The performance of compact energy storage is superior to SPC and most of the previously reported porous carbon materials (Fig. 6c) [15,16,18,21,45,68,75–82].

Considering that the specific surface area of CPC (2,007 m2 g−1) is higher than SPC (1,404 m2 g−1), the similar gravimetric capacitance of the two samples demonstrates that CPC possesses a relatively lower specific areal capacitance of 0.14 F m− 2 (capacitance per BET surface area) compared with SPC of 0.18 F m− 2. In addition, SPC shows a slightly superior rate performance at high current densities beyond 80 A g− 1. As CPC and SPC have similar microporous structure, the reason is ascribed to that SPC has a smaller particle size and rougher surface due to the serious foaming during activation, which is beneficial for the in-pore electrolyte infiltration and shortening the ion transport paths. To deep understand the mechanism, we measured the EIS plots and calculated the electrochemical kinetic parameters (Fig. 6d). The radii of the semicircles in the high frequency region and the slopes of the tails in low frequency region are ascribed to the electrical double-layer formation impendance (Ref) and the Warburg impendance (Zw), respectively. Both CPC and SPC show the comparable electric conductivity and mesopore volume. The similar tail slopes and Zw values indicate the similar ion migration rate of the two samples. This is because: (1) the interconnected porous structure originated from the homogeneous activation plays an important role for promoting the fast electrolyte ion migration in both samples; and (2) CPC shows a larger micropore size distribution from 0.68 to 1.27 nm than SPC with a dominated size of 0.59 nm, which can further improve the in-pore ion migration rate. However, SPC has a better electrical double-layer formation kinetics than CPC (lower Ref value). This proves that the loose structure and smaller particle size of SPC (SEM images in Fig. 1a3 and 1b3) can shorten the ion transport paths and enhance the active site utilization. The result also suggests that designing dense porous carbon with high conductivity and interconnected microporous structure to enable fast electron and ion transport kinetics, suitable particle size to promote efficient active site utilization, will be the promising way for further improving the compact and high-rate capacitive energy storage performance.

To achieve the demand of high-efficiency and low-cost energy storage, improving the total energy density through increasing the active mass loading on supercapacitor electrodes is crucial for practical application [83]. Therefore, we assembled the CPC//CPC symmetric supercapacitor with the commercial electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm− 2 to illustrate the application performance. The calculated volumetric capacitances of CPC at the mass loading of 2 mg cm− 2 are 331 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1 and 265 F cm− 3 at 20 A g− 1 (80% retention). When increasing the mass loading to 10 mg cm− 2, CPC shows a decreased capacitance of 303 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1 and 148 F cm− 3 at 20 A g− 1, but still much better than the commercial activated carbon (YP-50F, Fig. 7 a). The EIS result shows that the ion migration resistance is obviously increased with the rising of electrode mass loading (Zw increases from 0.76 to 3.90 Ω, Fig. 7b), indicating that the decreased capacitance and rate performance are mainly due to the partial block of ion transport channels and accessible interface of high-mass-loading electrodes. The energy and power densities are reflected in the Ragone plots (Fig. 7c and d). CPC//CPC supercapacitor at 2 mg cm− 2 exhibits high energy densities of 9.50 Wh kg− 1/11.50 Wh L−1 and 7.60 Wh kg− 1/9.20 Wh L−1 at the power densities of 247 W kg− 1/298 W L−1 and 4,469 W kg− 1/5,407 W L−1, respectively. Even at 10 mg cm− 2, CPC//CPC supercapacitor still maintains high energy densities of 8.68 Wh kg− 1/10.50 Wh L−1 at the power densities of 244 W kg− 1/295 Wh L−1, which are higher or comparable to other reported carbon materials [16,23,47,84–90]. Furthermore, the CPC//CPC supercapacitor also presents excellent cycle stability where no capacity fading is observed after 100,000 cycles at 10 A g− 1 (Fig. 7e).

In summary, we have proposed the preparation of a novel microporous carbon with the advantages of interconnected porous structure, large surface area (2,007 m2 g−1), and high compaction density (1.21 g cm− 3) through simple homogeneous activation of chitosan. The mechanism is revealed as the thermally induced Schiff base reaction between amino and aldehyde groups during pyrolysis/activation, leading to the formation of highly cross-linked and stable scaffold that can (1) inhibit the serious foaming phenomenon and (2) facilitate the cyclization and aromatization to form dense carbon scaffold. The sample shows high capacitances of 280 F g−1/339 F cm− 3 at 1 A g− 1 and remarkable rate performance with 58% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1 in three-electrode system. At the commercial mass loading of 10 mg cm− 2, the CPC//CPC symmetric supercapacitor delivers high energy density of 10.50 Wh L−1 and 5.13 Wh L−1 at the power density of 295 and 5,952 W L−1. Moreover, CPC//CPC supercapacitor shows good cycle stability with no capacitance fading after 100,000 cycles at 10 A g− 1, showing high potential for practical application. The thermally induced Schiff base reaction may provide new thoughts for designing other dense and functional carbon materials.
